examples.title: Waterfall Phase Execution Template
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the sequential execution of Waterfall phases with gate reviews
examples.code:
PROCEDURE WaterfallDevelopment()
 BEGIN
 // Phase 1: Requirements
 requirementsDoc ← ConductRequirementsGathering()
 IF NOT ReviewAndApprove(requirementsDoc) THEN
 REPEAT RequirementsGathering()
 UNTIL Approved
 ENDIF
 
 // Phase 2: Design
 designDoc ← CreateSystemDesign(requirementsDoc)
 IF NOT ReviewAndApprove(designDoc) THEN
 REPEAT SystemDesign()
 UNTIL Approved
 ENDIF
 
 // Phase 3: Implementation
 sourceCode ← ImplementSystem(designDoc)
 IF NOT ReviewAndApprove(sourceCode) THEN
 REPEAT Implementation()
 UNTIL Approved
 ENDIF
 
 // Phase 4: Testing
 testResults ← ExecuteTesting(sourceCode)
 IF NOT ReviewAndApprove(testResults) THEN
 REPEAT FixDefects()
 UNTIL AllTestsPass
 ENDIF
 
 // Phase 5: Deployment
 DeploySystem()
 
 // Phase 6: Maintenance
 WHILE SystemIsOperational DO
 HandleMaintenance()
 ENDWHILE
 END
examples.explanation: Demonstrates the gate review mechanism at each phase. The IF-THEN-REPEAT structure shows how Waterfall enforces completion criteria before phase transition. Returns to previous phases only occur through explicit 'repeat' loops triggered by review failures.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of phases (6)

examples.title: Change Request Processing in Waterfall
examples.description: Pseudocode showing the formal process for handling requirements changes
examples.code:
PROCEDURE HandleChangeRequest(changeRequest)
 BEGIN
 // Create formal change request
 impactAnalysis ← AnalyzeImpact(changeRequest)
 
 // Calculate cost and schedule impact
 estimatedCost ← impactAnalysis.costMultiplier * originalEstimate
 estimatedDelay ← CalculateScheduleImpact(changeRequest)
 
 // Present to Change Control Board
 decision ← CCB_Review(impactAnalysis)
 
 IF decision = APPROVED THEN
 // Must return to earlier phase
 RETURN_TO_PHASE_1() // Requirements
 RestartFullLifecycle(changeRequest)
 ELSE
 RejectChange(changeRequest)
 ContinueCurrentDevelopment()
 ENDIF
 END

FUNCTION CalculateScheduleImpact(change)
 BEGIN
 // In Waterfall, any change affects ALL subsequent phases
 phaseAffected ← DetermineEarliestAffectedPhase(change)
 remainingPhases ← TotalPhases - phaseAffected
 delay ← remainingPhases * AveragePhaseDuration
 RETURN delay
 END
examples.explanation: Illustrates the cascade effect of changes in Waterfall. Once a change is approved through Change Control Board (CCB), the project returns to Phase 1 (Requirements), causing maximum disruption. This formal process ensures changes are properly evaluated but demonstrates Waterfall's inflexibility.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for impact analysis, but O(n²) when cascade effects are considered

examples.title: Iterative Waterfall with Feedback Loops
examples.description: Modified Waterfall allowing controlled iteration between phases
examples.code:
PROCEDURE IterativeWaterfall()
 BEGIN
 currentPhase ← 1
 maxIterations ← 3
 
 WHILE currentPhase <= TotalPhases DO
 phaseOutput ← ExecutePhase(currentPhase)
 feedback ← ReviewAndGetFeedback(phaseOutput)
 
 iterationCount ← 1
 WHILE feedback.needsRevision AND iterationCount < maxIterations DO
 revisedOutput ← ReviseBasedOnFeedback(phaseOutput, feedback)
 feedback ← ReviewAndGetFeedback(revisedOutput)
 iterationCount ← iterationCount + 1
 ENDWHILE
 
 // Controlled feedback - can return to previous phase if critical
 IF feedback.criticalIssue THEN
 currentPhase ← feedback.targetPhase // Can go back
 ELSE
 currentPhase ← currentPhase + 1 // Move forward
 ENDIF
 ENDWHILE
 END
examples.explanation: Shows how Iterative Waterfall adds controlled feedback loops. Unlike pure Waterfall where returning to earlier phases is problematic, this allows limited iteration within a phase and controlled return to earlier phases for critical issues. Maintains structure while adding flexibility.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n = phases, m = max iterations per phase
